skill.name: toon-format
skill.description: Encode and validate structured data in TOON, a compact notation for LLM prompts and documentation datasets.
skill.summary: Convert structured data guidance into compact TOON format with validation rules and repair steps.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high and medium patterns, but most are Markdown syntax examples, inline code backticks, URLs, or sample hashes. The meaningful risk is that the skill asks agents to run npx, npm, and local Python repair scripts on user files, so publication is acceptable with a warning.

medium_findings.title: External validation and repair commands require review
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends npx, npm, and Python commands for validation and auto-fixing. These commands are not hidden or automatically executed by bundled code, but they can install packages or modify files when followed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in Markdown and serve a legitimate validation workflow. Risk remains because npx and Python repair scripts can execute code or alter files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation references
low_findings.description: The static network findings point to public TOON playground, specification, and source documentation URLs. No evidence found of credential exfiltration or background network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only as user-facing references and sample source metadata. I did not find code that sends data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography matches are sample hashes and prose
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations are descriptions, sample content_hash values, or ordinary TOON examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or custom cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain documentation text and short example identifiers. There is no executable cryptographic implementation in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Filesystem indicators are command examples
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings involve shell redirection to /dev/null and commands that process user-selected paths. They are visible examples, not stealth file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are not malicious by themselves, but they can overwrite or change project files when the user supplies a broad path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package execution through npx
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation workflow uses npx to execute the TOON CLI. This is useful, but marketplace users should understand that it may download or run package code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: npx execution is directly shown in command examples. The commands target a named public package and are not obfuscated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Auto-fix scripts can modify local files
dangerous_patterns.description: The auto-fix workflow tells users to run several Python scripts against a path. The referenced scripts are outside this skill, so their behavior must be trusted separately.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly instructs running local Python scripts on user-selected paths. The reviewed skill does not include those script bodies.

content.value_statement: JSON can be verbose when structured data is sent to Claude, Codex, or Claude Code. This skill guides compact TOON formatting, validation, and repair workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains TOON syntax for key-value data, arrays, tabular rows, and nested objects.
Shows when TOON is useful for repeated structured data and LLM transport.
Provides formatting rules for counts, indentation, semicolon separators, and blank row termination.
Documents validation with the official TOON CLI decode command.
Lists repair steps for common TOON errors such as comments, lists, pipes, and multiline strings.
limitations: It does not include an embedded TOON parser or validator.
It relies on external packages or local scripts for automated validation and repairs.
It is less suitable for deeply nested or non-uniform data.
It should not replace canonical JSON for tool-read configuration files.

use_cases.title: Prepare LLM datasets
use_cases.description: Convert repeated records into compact TOON before sending them to an AI model.

use_cases.title: Document structured indexes
use_cases.description: Create concise documentation indexes that stay readable while using fewer prompt tokens.

use_cases.title: Review TOON formatting
use_cases.description: Check counts, row indentation, separators, and blank lines before publishing data.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a small table
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this list of records into valid TOON. Use explicit counts and two-space row indentation.

prompt_templates.title: Check TOON syntax
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TOON text for syntax problems. Identify missing counts, row indentation errors, and missing blank lines.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize prompt data
prompt_templates.prompt: Restructure this JSON-like data for TOON. Prefer tabular arrays where the records share the same fields.

prompt_templates.title: Repair a complex file
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this TOON file, explain each format issue, and propose a corrected version that follows the skill rules.

output_examples.input: A user provides three product records with the same fields.
output_examples.output: The response returns a compact TOON table with a count, field names, and one row per product.

output_examples.input: A user asks why a TOON file fails validation.
output: The response lists each likely format issue in plain language.
It recommends fixes for indentation, counts, separators, or blank lines.

output_examples.input: A documentation team wants smaller index data for a prompt.
output_examples.output: The response suggests tabular TOON rows and explains which fields should stay in canonical JSON.

best_practices: Keep JSON as the source of truth and use TOON for prompt transport.
Use tabular arrays only when records share a consistent field set.
Validate generated TOON before using it in production prompts.
anti_patterns: Do not use TOON for deeply nested configuration read directly by tools.
Do not omit array counts or blank lines after tabular arrays.
Do not run auto-fix scripts on broad directories without reviewing changes.

faq.question: What is TOON?
faq.answer: TOON is a compact structured data notation designed to reduce prompt tokens while remaining readable.

faq.question: Does this skill validate TOON by itself?
faq.answer: No. It documents validation commands that use the official TOON CLI or related tooling.

faq.question: Can it replace JSON everywhere?
faq.answer: No. JSON is better for canonical storage, APIs, and configuration consumed by software tools.

faq.question: Why are array counts required?
faq.answer: Counts help readers and models detect missing rows or malformed arrays.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it recommends external commands and repair scripts.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: It is documented for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
